**Canberra Stadium**, commercially known as **GIO Stadium Canberra**, is a facility primarily used for [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") and [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") games, located adjacent to the [Australian Institute of Sport](/wiki/Australian_Institute_of_Sport "Australian Institute of Sport") in [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"), the capital of Australia. It is the largest sports venue by capacity in Canberra. The Canberra Stadium was previously known as **Bruce Stadium** and the National Athletics Stadium before its current name.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canberra_Stadium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The facility was designed by architect [Philip Cox](/wiki/Philip_Cox "Philip Cox")[[2]](#cite_note-2) and constructed by [Leighton Contractors](/wiki/CIMIC_Group "CIMIC Group").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It opened on 29 October 1977.[[4]](#cite_note-CamberraTimes291077-4)

In 1977, it was the venue for the [Pacific Conference Games](/wiki/Pacific_Conference_Games "Pacific Conference Games"), and was also the venue for the 4th [IAAF](/wiki/International_Association_of_Athletics_Federations "International Association of Athletics Federations") [World Cup in Athletics](/wiki/World_Cup_in_Athletics "World Cup in Athletics").[[4]](#cite_note-CamberraTimes291077-4) At the latter meet, the still-current world record for the women's 400m was recorded by [East German](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") [Marita Koch](/wiki/Marita_Koch "Marita Koch"), and a world record for the women's 4 × 100 m relay was set by East Germany, which stood until the [2012 London Olympic Games](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics").

In the late 1980s, the running track was removed and the warm-up track next door upgraded. New offices, seating, and [photo-finish](/wiki/Photo_finish "Photo finish") facilities were added. In the [1990 NSWRL season](/wiki/1990_NSWRL_season "1990 NSWRL season"), the [reigning NSWRL premiers](/wiki/1989_NSWRL_season "1989 NSWRL season") the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") moved to Bruce Stadium from [Seiffert Oval](/wiki/Seiffert_Oval "Seiffert Oval") in [Queanbeyan](/wiki/Queanbeyan "Queanbeyan"), their home ground since entering the [New South Wales Rugby League](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League "New South Wales Rugby League") in [1982](/wiki/1982_NSWRFL_season "1982 NSWRFL season"). The Raiders won their second straight premiership in 1990.

The removal of the athletics track meant that [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football") games, more specifically those of the [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League") (AFL), could be played at the ground, resulting in pre-season matches being scheduled as early as 1990.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1995, an AFL match for premiership points was contested between the [West Coast Eagles](/wiki/West_Coast_Eagles "West Coast Eagles") and [Fitzroy](/wiki/Fitzroy_Football_Club "Fitzroy Football Club"). There were also a number of pre-season AFL games played at the venue, mostly featuring the [Sydney Swans](/wiki/Sydney_Swans "Sydney Swans").

Also around that time, a [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") pitch was placed in the centre of the ground as an experiment, and a day/night [one-day cricket](/wiki/Limited_overs_cricket "Limited overs cricket") match was played between two local teams before a small crowd. Regular cricket matches on the ground did not eventuate.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/A_league_pre_season_sydney_vs_newcastle.jpg/250px-A_league_pre_season_sydney_vs_newcastle.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_league_pre_season_sydney_vs_newcastle.jpg)

[Sydney FC](/wiki/Sydney_FC "Sydney FC") playing [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_United_Jets "Newcastle United Jets") at Canberra Stadium in 2006

Further renovations occurred in 1997, in preparation for staging soccer matches as part of the [2000 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics") in Sydney,[[6]](#cite_note-6) which shrank the size of the playing field, preventing any future Australian rules football games being played there. The final cost of the renovations was more than seven times what had been originally anticipated by the Territory government of the time, and the subsequent controversy ended the career of then Chief Minister [Kate Carnell](/wiki/Kate_Carnell "Kate Carnell").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[*[circular reference](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Wikipedia_and_sources_that_mirror_or_use_it "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] During the lead-up, unseasonal snow fell on 28 May 2000, during a match between the Raiders and the Wests Tigers, the only such occasion in [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") history, with the snow causing frost damage to the turf intended for the [Olympic soccer](/wiki/Football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Football at the Summer Olympics") tournament.

Olympic soccer in 2000 initiated a stadium facelift, converting the playing surface from oval to rectangular and bringing the crowd closer to the action.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It is now an all-seater rectangular stadium with two main grandstands on either side of the playing field. The major outcome of that revamp was that the stadium could no longer host AFL games. All top-class cricket and Australian rules football games in Canberra are now staged at the 15,000-capacity [Manuka Oval](/wiki/Manuka_Oval "Manuka Oval").

A [2008 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2008_Rugby_League_World_Cup_Group_B "2008 Rugby League World Cup Group B") game between [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_league_team "Scotland national rugby league team") and [France](/wiki/France_national_rugby_league_team "France national rugby league team") was played at Canberra Stadium, the first ever [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") test played at the venue. France defeated Scotland 36–16.

In 2009, there was an [A-League bid from Canberra](/wiki/Canberra_A-League_Bid "Canberra A-League Bid") that, if successful would have seen an [A-League](/wiki/A-League "A-League") soccer team play at the stadium starting with the 2010–11 season. However, the League decided that the new teams would be [Sydney Rovers FC](/wiki/Sydney_Rovers_FC "Sydney Rovers FC") (which dissolved due to financial problems) and [Melbourne Heart FC](/wiki/Melbourne_Heart_FC "Melbourne Heart FC").

To coincide with Canberra's 100th birthday celebrations, the stadium was chosen to host the [2013](/wiki/2013_Anzac_Test "2013 Anzac Test") Rugby League [Anzac Test](/wiki/Anzac_Test "Anzac Test") between [Australia](/wiki/Australian_Kangaroos "Australian Kangaroos") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_Kiwis "New Zealand Kiwis"). On 19 April 2013 in what was the first game ever played by the Kangaroos in Australia's capital city, a crowd of 25,628 saw Australia defeat their Trans-Tasman rivals 32–12.

In January 2014, Canberra Stadium was rebranded GIO Stadium Canberra as part of a [naming rights](/wiki/Naming_rights "Naming rights") deal with [GIO Insurance](/wiki/GIO_Insurance "GIO Insurance").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

A new video screen was installed at the southern end of the stadium in March 2020.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canberra_Stadium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ownership")]

The stadium is currently owned by the [Australian Government](/wiki/Government_of_Australia "Government of Australia") through the [Australian Sports Commission](/wiki/Australian_Sports_Commission "Australian Sports Commission") and leased to the [Government of the Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Government_of_the_Australian_Capital_Territory "Government of the Australian Capital Territory"). While the current lease is due to expire in 2010, the ACT Government is seeking ownership of the stadium through a land transfer with the Australian Government.

## Seating and capacity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canberra_Stadium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Seating and capacity")]

Capacity is a nominal all-seated 25,011, the largest crowd being 28,753 for the 2004 Super 12 Final. The main grandstand is named after Canberra Raiders and [Australian](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") rugby league player [Mal Meninga](/wiki/Mal_Meninga "Mal Meninga"), and a statue of another Raiders and Australian league representative [Laurie Daley](/wiki/Laurie_Daley "Laurie Daley") adorns the main grandstand entrance.

[![Picture of the Gregan Larkham Grandstand](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Gregan_Larkham_stand.JPG/250px-Gregan_Larkham_stand.JPG)](/wiki/File:Gregan_Larkham_stand.JPG)

Unveiling of the Gregan-Larkham stand at Canberra Stadium on 28 April 2007.

The eastern grandstand was named the Gregan/Larkham Grandstand on 28 April 2007, after [Brumbies](/wiki/Brumbies "Brumbies") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team") rugby union greats [George Gregan](/wiki/George_Gregan "George Gregan") and [Stephen Larkham](/wiki/Stephen_Larkham "Stephen Larkham"). Both ended their international careers after the [2007 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2007_Rugby_World_Cup "2007 Rugby World Cup") as the two most-capped players in Wallabies history (at that time), with Gregan at [a world-record](/wiki/List_of_rugby_union_Test_caps_leaders "List of rugby union Test caps leaders") 139 and Larkham at 102.

## Possible replacement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canberra_Stadium&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Possible replacement")]

Whilst the stadium suits the needs of its two current primary tenants, as of 2017 it is the smallest Super Rugby stadium and only a medium-sized NRL venue. The stadium itself is approaching 35 years old, and despite modernisations over the years is lacking in certain amenities for fans – especially covered seating.

Additionally, Australia had [bid](/wiki/Australia_2022_FIFA_World_Cup_bid "Australia 2022 FIFA World Cup bid") for the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup") and Canberra Stadium does not meet the necessary criteria to host matches. As such, the ACT Government launched a study examining the upgrade or replacement of Canberra Stadium, with options ranging from increasing capacity and enclosing the current facility, to completely re-configuring the current stadium to an oval for cricket and Australian rules football and building a state of the art rectangular facility next door.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Citing costs of building multiple facilities as an issue, ACT Sports Minister [Andrew Barr](/wiki/Andrew_Barr "Andrew Barr") indicated his preference would be a 'super stadium' built with World Cup standard facilities and capacity, able to be reduced to approximately 30,000 seats after the event. Such a facility would have to incorporate [movable seating](/wiki/Movable_seating "Movable seating") in order to accommodate all of the major Australian sporting codes.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The official bid for the 2022 World Cup indicated that the 'super stadium' plan was unlikely and the original plan of a new rectangular stadium built next door to the current stadium, with the existing facility re-configured for oval field sports, was considered to be the likely outcome.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

After the failed World Cup bid a new rectangular covered stadium was proposed for Canberra.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In 2013 the ACT government announced plans to build a 30,000 covered (with a roof similar to [Forsyth Barr Stadium](/wiki/Forsyth_Barr_Stadium "Forsyth Barr Stadium")) rectangular stadium in the city on the shores of Lake Burley Griffin. It would be part of a 15-year significant redevelopment of the foreshore which extends the city to the Eastern Basin. Along with the stadium, as part of the redevelopment there would be apartments, a convention centre and an urban beach.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Plans to build a new stadium have, however, been put on hold indefinitely due to the need for funds to compensate local residents over an asbestos home insulation debacle.[[17]](#cite_note-auto-17) Plans to construct the new stadium have since been pushed back by a decade.[[17]](#cite_note-auto-17)

* In 1990, the stadium hosted an [International Rules](/wiki/International_Rules "International Rules") match (a combination of [Gaelic football](/wiki/Gaelic_football "Gaelic football") and [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football")) between [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_international_rules_football_team "Ireland international rules football team") and [Australia](/wiki/Australian_international_rules_football_team "Australian international rules football team"). Ireland defeated Australia 52–31 in front of 7,000 fans.

* In [1995](/wiki/1995_AFL_season "1995 AFL season"), the [AFL](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League")'s ailing [Fitzroy Football Club](/wiki/Fitzroy_Football_Club "Fitzroy Football Club") played one home game against the [West Coast Eagles](/wiki/West_Coast_Eagles "West Coast Eagles") at the venue. West Coast defeated Fitzroy 12.10 (82) to 8.6 (54) in front of 11,282 on 27 May.
* The 2nd leg of the [1996 OFC Nations Cup](/wiki/1996_OFC_Nations_Cup "1996 OFC Nations Cup") Final
* [Super 12](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") [Rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") Final [2000](/wiki/2000_Super_12_season "2000 Super 12 season"), [2001](/wiki/2001_Super_12_season "2001 Super 12 season"), [2004](/wiki/2004_Super_12_season "2004 Super 12 season")

* Four group matches from the [2003 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2003_Rugby_World_Cup "2003 Rugby World Cup").
* The 2008 Pacific Schools Games
* One Group Match from the [2008 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2008_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2008 Rugby League World Cup").
* On 19 April 2013 the Stadium hosted the [2013 Anzac Test](/wiki/2013_Anzac_Test "2013 Anzac Test"), the first time the Kangaroos had played a test in Canberra. Canberra Stadium was awarded the test as part of Canberra's centenary celebrations.
* [Kanga Cup](/wiki/Kanga_Cup "Kanga Cup") International youth soccer tournament.
* On 5 May 2017 the Stadium hosted the [2017 Anzac Test](/wiki/2017_Anzac_Test "2017 Anzac Test"). This was the last [Anzac Test](/wiki/Anzac_Test "Anzac Test") to be played.
* Hosted three Group Matches from the [2017 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2017_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2017 Rugby League World Cup") including the first Australia vs France test in Australia since [1994](/wiki/1994_French_rugby_league_Oceania_tour "1994 French rugby league Oceania tour").
